segments.purgatorio-5-g61: Guarda is an imperative singular ("look/see") addressing the addressee. s’ is a poetic contraction of se (if/whether) introducing the subordinate clause. Alcun di noi = "anyone of us" (alcun = someone/any), unqua is an archaic adverb meaning "ever," and vedesti is the passato remoto, 2nd person singular of vedere ("you saw"), placed at the end of the clause as is common in Italian subordinate clauses.
segments.purgatorio-5-g62: Sì che introduces a result/purpose clause ("so that/so as to"). Di lui di là stacks two prepositional phrases: di lui = "concerning him" and di là = "from there/over there" (here, the other place/world). Novella (here: "news, a report") is the direct object; porti is 2nd person singular and in this context expresses the hoped-for bringing of news (a present form rendered as a potential result/purpose — in poetry it reads like "may you bring" or "that you bring").
segments.purgatorio-5-g63: Deh is an interjection of appeal or entreaty ("ah, pray, please") used to soften or urge what follows.
segments.purgatorio-5-g64: Perché is the interrogative "why." Vai is 2nd person singular present of andare ("you go"); here it forms a direct question asking the reason for the addressee's movement ("why are you going?").
segments.purgatorio-5-g65: Deh is the same pleading interjection as above ("ah/please").
segments.purgatorio-5-g66: Perché introduces the question "why." Non is the negation "not." T’ is the clitic for ti (reflexive/oblique "yourself/you") elided before a vowel; arresti is 2nd person singular present of arrestare/arrestarsi (here reflexive: "you stop/you stay"), so non t’arresti? = "why do you not stop?" The subject (tu) is omitted as usual in Italian.
segments.purgatorio-5-g67: Noi is the subject pronoun 'we'. Fummo is the passato remoto (perfective past) of essere, 'we were'. The structure 'fummo ... morti' uses the copula plus past participle (morti) as a stative/result: 'we were dead.' Tutti già and per forza are adverbial modifiers: 'tutti già' = 'all already', and 'per forza' = idiomatically 'by force / forcibly' (indicating violent death). Word order is standard: subject–verb–predicative adjective with adverbs inserted.
segments.purgatorio-5-g68: E = 'and' linking the two coordinated facts. Peccatori is a nominative/subject complement meaning 'sinners'. Infino a l’ultima ora is a temporal phrase: 'infino a' = 'until', 'l’ultima ora' = 'the last hour' (i.e. up to the final moment). The whole segment continues and specifies that they remained sinners until the very end.
segments.purgatorio-5-g69: Quivi = 'there' (locative). Lume del ciel is the subject phrase 'the light of heaven' (lume = light, del ciel = of heaven). Ne is the clitic particle referring back to the preceding matter ('of it' / 'of that fact'). Fece is passato remoto of fare, used here causatively: 'made' or 'caused'. Accorti is a past-participle used adjectivally: 'aware' or 'alert'. The construction 'lume del ciel ne fece accorti' = 'the light of heaven made us aware of it.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g70: Sì che is a subordinating connective meaning 'so that' or 'and so'. It introduces the result clause that follows. Here it links the previous statement (being made aware) to the consequence (their repentance and departure from life).
segments.purgatorio-5-g71: Pentendo e perdonando are gerunds expressing attendant circumstances or the means by which the result occurred: 'repenting and (being) forgiven.' Pentendo is straightforward gerund of 'pentere' = 'repenting'. Perdonando is formally the active gerund of 'perdonare' ('forgiving'), but here it carries the sense of 'having been forgiven' or 'being pardoned' (a common Medieval/poetic usage where the passive/resultive sense is implied). The two gerunds together describe what happened to them as they left life.
segments.purgatorio-5-g72: Fora here is an archaic/formal adverb meaning 'out' or 'forth' (from foras); it introduces the verb phrase that follows. Di vita = 'from life' (prepositional complement). Uscimmo is passato remoto of uscire, 'we went out / we left'. A Dio pacificati is a prepositional phrase with a past participle used adjectivally: 'to God reconciled' or 'reconciled with God' — pacificati agrees with the plural subject. Overall: 'we went forth from life reconciled to God.' Note: 'fora' is archaic and is not listed in the provided senses, so its sense is flagged below accordingly.
segments.purgatorio-5-g73: Che introduces a final explanatory/causal clause ('for' / 'because' / 'who'), linking back to the previous statement. Del disio = 'from / because of the desire' (del = di + il). Di sé veder: disio takes a following infinitive phrase introduced by di; 'di sé veder' = 'to see himself' ('sé' = himself). N’ is the clitic ne ('of it' / 'thereof' / 'about it') and attaches before the verb accora. Accora is an archaic verb meaning 'to sadden / distress' (third person form here). So the clause says that (him/them) are/ were distressed/grieved by the desire to see himself; the exact referent (God or the souls) is contextually determined but grammatically the clause modifies the foregoing idea.
segments.purgatorio-5-g74: "E io:" is simply the speaker's turn-taking: E (and) connects his speech to what came before, and io (I) is a stressed subject pronoun placed before the colon to introduce what he says.
segments.purgatorio-5-g75: Perché here means "because/for," introducing the reason; ne' is the contracted form of ne + the article and means "in/on/among" (here "in"); vostri visi is "your faces" (vostri = possessive adjective, visi = faces); guati (plural of guato) means "looks/ga z es/watches" and modifies "visi" — literal order "in your faces' looks."
segments.purgatorio-5-g76: Non is the sentential negation "not"; riconosco is present indicative first-person singular of riconoscere, "I recognize"; alcun is an indefinite pronoun here used after negation to mean "anyone/no one" (negative force: "no one").
segments.purgatorio-5-g77: Ma "but" introduces a contrast. The sequence s’ a voi piace is an older contraction of se a voi piace, "if it pleases you" (s’ = se "if"); cosa ch’io possa is an object clause: cosa "anything/something" + ch’ (che) "that" + io possa, present subjunctive of potere (possa) because the existence of such a thing is conditional/uncertain — "anything that I may be able (to do)."
segments.purgatorio-5-g78: Spiriti is the vocative plural "spirits," here addressing them; ben nati is a compound adjectival phrase "well-born" (ben = "well" modifying the past participle nati = "born"). The phrase is a respectful form of address: "well-born spirits."
segments.purgatorio-5-g79: Voi dite is present indicative, second person plural: voi (you, vocative/direct address) + dite (you say/tell). In context it functions as an exhortation: "you say (it)" — i.e. tell me what that is.
segments.purgatorio-5-g80: E io farò is "and I will do" (farò future of fare); per quella pace = "for that peace" or "for the sake of that peace" (per indicating purpose or motive); che introduces the relative clause describing that peace.
segments.purgatorio-5-g81: Dietro a’ piedi is a phrase meaning "behind/after the feet" (dietro = "behind/after," a = preposition); di sì fatta guida literally "of so-made guide" is an archaic, inverted way to say "of such a guide" (sì = "such/so," fatta = past participle "made", guida = "guide"). Together: "which, following at the feet of such a guide."
segments.purgatorio-5-g82: Di mondo in mondo is an idiomatic iterated phrase "from world to world" (di = "from/of," mondo in mondo = "world in world" = "world to world"). Cercar is the archaic infinitive form (modern cercare) "to seek," and mi si face is an impersonal/periphrastic construction: si face (from fare) + infinitive + clitic mi means "it makes me seek" or "I am made/compelled to seek." So the clause means "which makes me seek from world to world."
segments.purgatorio-5-g1: Io is the subject pronoun 'I'. era is the imperfect (passato remoto/imperf.) form of essere used here as a past copula 'was'. già is an adverb 'already'. da + quell' ombre: da is the preposition 'from' and quell' ombre ('those shades') is the object/starting point. partito is a past participle here meaning 'departed/left' but its lemma senses list doesn't include this verbal sense (see senses for index).
segments.purgatorio-5-g2: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. seguitava is the imperfect 'was following' (ongoing past action) of seguitare. l' orma (here l'orme) is the definite article + plural noun 'the footprints/traces'. del = di + il 'of the'. mio duca = 'my guide/duke'; here duca functions as 'guide'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g3: quando is the temporal conjunction 'when'. di retro a me is an idiomatic locative 'from behind me' with di indicating origin/position, retro 'behind', a marking the indirect relation and me 'me'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g4: drizzando is a present participle (gerund-like use) meaning 'straightening/raising/pointing' and describes a simultaneous action; l' is the article 'the'. dito is 'finger' but the supplied lemma/senses appear mismatched, so note the participle governs the noun 'the finger' meaning 'pointing the finger'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g5: una is an indefinite pronoun here 'someone' (one of them). gridò is passato remoto (or passato) 'cried out/ cried' and reports the single instance of shouting.
segments.purgatorio-5-g6: This is the reported exclamation. Ve' is an archaic/poetic imperative/behold ('look!'), che introduces a subordinate clause 'that/does', non is negation, par is third-person singular present 'seems', che introduces the content clause, luca is an old form 'to shine' (inflected here), lo raggio = 'the ray', da sinistra = 'from the left', a quel di sotto is a fixed locution 'toward that lower place' (quel di sotto = 'that one below').
segments.purgatorio-5-g7: e = 'and'. come introduces a manner clause 'how/just as'. vivo is an adjective 'alive' or 'livelily' qualifying the preceding notion. par is again 'seems'. che introduces the subordinate clause with si conduca: reflexive si with conduca (present subjunctive form) gives an impersonal/reflexive sense 'it seems to conduct itself / to be guided' — subjunctive here expresses reported appearance.
segments.purgatorio-5-g8: Sentence-final punctuation: a period marking the end of the preceding line/sentence.
segments.purgatorio-5-g9: Li occhi is the noun phrase 'the eyes' with the archaic article li. rivolsi is the first person singular passato remoto of rivolgere here meaning 'I turned' (physically turned). al suon di questo motto = 'at the sound of this utterance/phrase' (al = a + il, suon = sound, motto = word/utterance).
segments.purgatorio-5-g10: e = 'and'. vidile is a fused form of vidi + le (I saw them/her); here it means 'I saw them'. guardar is an infinitive 'to look' that follows vidile and takes a direct object/compliment. per maraviglia = 'out of/amazed by wonder' (per = because of/through). pur me = 'even at me' (pur = even; me = me).
segments.purgatorio-5-g11: pur me is a short emphatic repetition meaning 'even me'/'even at me' — pur = still/even and me = me; repeated for emphasis in the verse.
segments.purgatorio-5-g12: e = 'and'. 'l lume = the light; ch' (che) = 'that/which'; era is the imperfect (or passato) 'was'; rotto is past participle 'broken' (here 'the light that was broken' or 'the light that had been extinguished/interrupted').
segments.purgatorio-5-g13: Perché is the interrogative 'why'. l' animo tuo = 'your spirit/mind' with the definite article. tanto modifies the degree 'so much/so'. s' impiglia is the reflexive construction (si + impiglia) meaning 'becomes entangled/gets caught' — impiglia is present tense (or present-subjunctive form in speech) describing the state causing the problem.
segments.purgatorio-5-g14: disse is the passato remoto 'said' (narrative past). l' maestro = 'the master' (the speaker identified as the teacher/guide). The comma sets off the reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-5-g15: che introduces a result/consequence clause 'that'. l' andare = 'the going/walking' with article + infinitive acting as a noun. allenti is present subjunctive or present indicative (here rhetorical) from allentare meaning 'to loosen/relax' — used figuratively 'that it slackens' or 'makes your pace slow'. The whole clause asks why the soul's entanglement makes his walking slacken.
segments.purgatorio-5-g115: This segment is only punctuation (a period). It marks a pause or end of a line and has no grammatical content.
segments.purgatorio-5-g116: An interjection/exclamation expressing emotion or surprise; kept in quotes as direct speech. It functions alone as the whole sentence.
segments.purgatorio-5-g117: A single comma segment providing punctuation between the exclamation and the reported speech; it has no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-5-g118: Rispuos’ is a contracted, archaic past form: rispuos’ stands for rispose (he replied), the final vowel elided for metrical reasons; elli is an older third-person pronoun meaning 'he' (here 'he replied').
segments.purgatorio-5-g119: Word order is inverted: a piè del Casentino sets the prepositional place ('at the foot of the Casentino') up front; traversa is the verb agreeing with un’acqua (an inverted subject after the verb), meaning 'flows across / runs'; un’acqua is 'a water/stream'; c’ ha is a contracted form of che ha ('that has'); nome l’Archiano = 'is called the Archiano' (literally 'has the name Archiano').
segments.purgatorio-5-g120: che is a relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing the relative clause; sovra l’Ermo nasce literally 'springs above the Ermo' — nasce is present tense 'springs up/originate' used here to describe the river's source; Apennino names the mountain range (in + place).
segments.purgatorio-5-g121: Là 've: Là = 'there; ’ve (an archaic/poetic form of ove) = 'where'. vocabol suo = 'its name/word' (vocabol = 'word'/'term'); diventa vano = 'becomes vain/empty/meaningless' — diventa is present 'becomes'. Altogether: 'There where its name becomes meaningless.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g122: arriva’ io: the apostrophe marks elision of the final vowel in an older past-tense form (arriva' stands for arrivai/arrivai), so this is first-person past 'I arrived/I came'; forato is a past participle/adjectival form 'pierced' or 'bored through'; ne la gola = 'into the throat' (ne = in/into here).
segments.purgatorio-5-g123: fuggendo = gerund 'fleeing'; a piede = 'on foot' or 'by foot' (a + piede expresses manner); e sanguinando = 'and (while) bleeding' (sanguinando gerund); il piano = 'the plain' or 'level ground' (piano = plain/flat ground), so the phrase describes fleeing on foot while bleeding across the plain.
segments.purgatorio-5-g16: This is an interrogative clause. Che is an interrogative 'what' at the front; ti is the indirect object 'to you'; fa is the 3rd-person singular present of fare meaning 'causes/makes'; ciò che is a correlative construction 'that which/what'; quivi is an archaic locative 'there/here in this place'; si pispiglia is an impersonal or reflexive passive construction (si + verb) meaning 'is whispered/muttered among themselves'. The whole asks what causes you to be affected by what is being murmured here.
segments.purgatorio-5-g17: Vien is an imperative/present-form of venire meaning 'come' (here as a command), followed by dietro a me 'behind me' = 'follow me'. The phrase functions as an instruction to follow.
segments.purgatorio-5-g18: Lascia is a 2nd-person imperative/present of lasciare meaning 'let' or 'leave'; it takes the bare infinitive dir (old form of dire) as its complement 'let (them) say/let speak'. Le genti is the people and is the logical subject of dir (the people who will speak).
segments.purgatorio-5-g19: Sta is the 2nd-person imperative/present of stare 'stand/stay'; come introduces a simile 'like'; torre ferma is a nominal phrase 'a firm/steady tower' (ferma an adjective). Together: 'stand like a steady tower.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g20: Che is a relative pronoun 'which/that'; non crolla is present indicative negative 'does not fall/collapse'; già mai here intensifies temporality ('ever' in older usage); la cima 'the summit/top' is the subject and per soffiar di venti gives the cause/instrument 'by the blowing of winds'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g21: Ché is causal 'for/because'; sempre 'always'; l’omo 'the man'; in cui is a relative 'in whom'; pensier rampolla is a nominal phrase where rampolla ('a young shoot/offspring') metaphorically modifies pensier—'in whom a thought sprouts'; sovra pensier 'upon thought' indicates one thought coming upon another.
segments.purgatorio-5-g22: Da sé 'away from himself' (da + reflexive sé); dilunga is 3rd-person singular present of dilungere meaning here 'distances, scatters, draws away' (i.e. separates); il segno 'the mark/sign' is the direct object—literally 'he draws the sign away from himself.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g23: Perché 'because'; la foga 'the ardour/impetuosity' (la = the); l'un de l'altro 'one of the other' (lit. one from the other); insolla is a rare verb meaning literally 'to expose to the sun' or 'to sun/heat' used figuratively here: 'ardour bakes/heats one upon the other', i.e. one ardour affects the next. The clause gives the causal image for the mental distraction.
segments.purgatorio-5-g24: Che here is an exclamatory/interrogative 'what' (what could I...); potea is the imperfect of potere 'could' expressing past ability; io is the subject pronoun 'I'; ridir is the infinitive 'to reply/say again' (ridir = rispondere/replicate). The clause asks what the speaker could say in response.
segments.purgatorio-5-g25: Se non 'if not' / 'except'; Io vegno is present tense 'I come' (vegno = venire). The whole is the brief answer the speaker could give: 'If not “I come?”' (i.e. nothing else to say).
segments.purgatorio-5-g26: Dissilo is a contracted past form: disse + lo → 'he said it.' In narrative it reports that the master uttered those words.
segments.purgatorio-5-g27: Alquanto 'somewhat' or 'a little'; del (di + il) 'of the'; color 'colour/tint' (here 'the hue/tinge' of something) with consperso (past participle of conspersare) 'sprinkled over/scattered about' describing that colour; che introduces a relative clause 'which'; fa 'makes' (3rd-sing. present of fare); l’uom 'the man'; di perdon 'of/unto pardon' (i.e. 'worthy of pardon'); talvolta 'sometimes'; degno 'worthy/deserving'. The phrase describes a subtle appearance that can make a man at times seem worthy of pardon.
segments.purgatorio-5-g28: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' '’ntanto' is a poetic contraction of 'intanto' meaning 'meanwhile.' 'per la costa di traverso' uses 'per' to indicate movement along/through the mountain's side (here 'costa' = slope), with 'di traverso' meaning 'crosswise/athwart' — together: 'across the slope.' 'venivan' is the archaic imperfect third-person plural (modern 'venivano'), so 'they were coming' (background/ongoing past). 'genti' is plural 'people.' 'innanzi a noi' = 'in front of us' (innanzi + a + noi). 'un poco' = 'a little' (modifying how far ahead they were).
segments.purgatorio-5-g29: 'cantando' is a gerund (present participle) meaning 'singing' or 'chanting,' giving the accompanying action. 'Miserere' is the invocation/psalm title (literally 'Have mercy'). The repeated prepositional phrase 'a verso a verso' means 'verse by verse' or 'line after line,' with 'a' used idiomatically to mark repetition and 'verso' here meaning 'verse/line.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g30: 'Quando' = 'when.' 's’accorser' is an archaic contracted form of 'si accorsero' (third-person plural perfect), here meaning 'they noticed/perceived.' The subordinating 'ch’' = 'che' introduces the clause. 'i’' is an archaic form of 'io' (I). 'non dava loco' uses the imperfect 'dava' ('gave') with 'loco' ('place/space') to mean 'did not give room' or 'did not yield space.' 'per lo mio corpo' = 'for/by reason of my body' (i.e., with regard to my body). 'al trapassar d’i raggi' literally 'to/at the passing of the rays' — 'trapassar' (infinitive) = 'to pass through' and 'raggi' = 'rays' (light); the whole phrase means the rays passing through or past them. Grammatically, the imperfect (dava) describes a simultaneous/background situation relative to the noticing.
segments.purgatorio-5-g31: Infinitive 'mutar' (archaic for 'mutare') means 'to change.' 'lor' is the archaic clitic for 'loro' ('their') and modifies 'canto' ('song'). 'in un «oh!»' shows the result of the change: into an exclamation 'oh!' — 'in' marks the resulting state. The clause is terse because the subject ('they') is carried over from the previous line.
segments.purgatorio-5-g32: 'lungo e roco' are adjectives describing the sound just mentioned: 'long' (in duration) and 'hoarse/rasping.' In poetry adjectives can follow the noun/exclamation they modify; here they summarize the new cry's quality.
segments.purgatorio-5-g33: A short phrase meaning 'and two of them' — 'e' = and; 'due' (present in the text) is 'two'; 'di loro' = 'of them.' This simply identifies two individuals among the group.
segments.purgatorio-5-g34: 'in forma di' = 'in the form/shape of.' 'messaggi' here denotes emissaries/agents sent to speak (literally 'messages' or 'dispatches' in the lemma list); contextually they appear as messengers. The prepositional phrase marks their appearance/guise.
segments.purgatorio-5-g35: 'corsero' is the passato remoto of 'correre' (they ran/rushed). 'incontr’ a noi' (contracted 'incontra') means 'toward us'/'to meet us.' 'e dimandarne' combines 'dimandare' (to ask) with the clitic '-ne' meaning 'about it' or 'of it' — 'they ran to us and asked about it.' The infinitive/clitic form indicates the action of asking information, with the referent supplied by context.
segments.purgatorio-5-g36: This direct speech: 'Di vostra condizion' = 'about your condition' (Di + noun). 'fatene' is the imperative form 'fatene' = 'do/make of it' (fatene = fate + ne; here 'ne' = of it/them), used idiomatically as 'let them know' or 'inform them.' 'saggi' is plural of 'saggio' meaning 'wise' or 'made wise' — the idiom 'fatene saggi' means 'make them wise' (i.e., inform them).
segments.purgatorio-5-g37: 'E' = 'and.' '’l' is the elided article 'il' + apostrophe (poetic elision) and 'mio maestro' = 'my master/teacher/guide' (Virgil). The short segment names the speaker ('my master:').
segments.purgatorio-5-g38: 'Voi potete' = 'you can' (present indicative of 'potere' — ability/permission). 'andarne' = 'andare' + clitic '-ne' (here roughly 'go' or 'go away/dispatch yourself to them'); the infinitive after 'potete' expresses what they may do. 'e ritrarre' — 'ritrarre' (here used in the sense 'to report/tell' rather than literal 'portray'; infinitive joined by conjunction). 'a color che vi mandaro' = 'to those who sent you' (a + 'color' = to those; 'che' introduces the relative clause; 'vi' = to you; 'mandaro' is an archaic past 'mandarono' = 'they sent'). The final clause 'che ’l corpo di costui è vera carne' is reported content: 'that this man's body is true/real flesh' — 'vera' before 'carne' emphasizes reality/authenticity. Grammatically, the second-person deontic/modal phrase + infinitives instruct the messengers what to do, and the subordinate 'che' clauses quote what they should report.
segments.purgatorio-5-g101: Corsi is the past-historic (preterite) first-person singular of correre (‘to run’), typical in narrative poetry; the subject 'io' is omitted because it is expressed by the verb. 'al palude' is a prepositional phrase (a + il contracted to al) functioning as the goal of motion: 'to the marsh.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g102: This clause lists the agents that acted on the speaker. The coordinated nominal subject 'le cannucce e ’l braco' ('the reeds and the hound') is followed by an archaic/poetic verb form: 'm’impigliar' stands for a past action meaning 'they entangled me' (the -ono ending of the third-person plural is omitted by poetic truncation). The clitic m’ = mi marks the affected person 'me', 'sì ch’i’ caddi' = 'so that I fell' (sì…ch’ introduces the result clause), with i’ an abbreviated form of io.
segments.purgatorio-5-g103: 'e lì vid’ io' places the action in the same scene: vid' is the poetic past-historic (shortened vidi) and is placed before the explicit subject io for emphasis. 'de le mie vene farsi in terra laco' uses reflexive/passive farsi + infinitive to mean 'to be made/turned into' (my veins being the source), with de marking source ('from my veins') and 'in terra laco' indicating result/location: 'a pool on the ground.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g104: Poi is a temporal/consecutive connector 'then/afterwards'. Disse is the past-historic (third-person singular) of dire: 'said'. 'un altro' is the subject (another person) introduced after the verb, a common narrative ordering.
segments.purgatorio-5-g105: Deh is an interjection (an urgent plea, 'ah' or 'oh, please') used to open the direct address; it marks entreaty and sets the tone of the following conditional wish.
segments.purgatorio-5-g106: This is a conditional/wish clause. 'se' (if) introduces the condition; 'quel disio' is the subject ('that desire'); 'si compia' is present subjunctive (compia) with the impersonal/reflexive si used to express a wish or hoped-for fulfillment ('may ... be fulfilled'). 'che ti tragge a l’alto monte' is a relative clause describing 'quel disio': che = 'that', ti = object 'you', tragge is present (here 'draws' or 'pulls') and a + l' + alto monte = 'to the high mountain.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g107: 'con buona pïetate' is an adverbial phrase 'with good pity/compassion' (the diaeresis on ï signals vowel separation). 'aiuta' is a second-person imperative ('help!') addressed to the addressee; 'il mio' is elliptical for 'my [soul/cause]' (the noun is omitted but understood), i.e. 'help mine.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g108: Io fui is the emphatic subject pronoun plus past-historic 'I was' (fui). 'di Montefeltro' expresses origin or family affiliation ('of/from Montefeltro') and identifies the speaker's provenance.
segments.purgatorio-5-g109: io son is a present-tense identification ('I am'), with the archaic short form son for modern sono; Bonconte is the speaker's name. The explicit io adds emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-5-g110: This clause has the nominative 'Giovanna o altri' ('Giovanna or others') as subject; 'non ha di me cura' literally 'have no care for me' — 'ha' is present-tense avere used in the sense 'to have/care' and 'di me cura' = 'care about me'. The negative non negates the clause.
segments.purgatorio-5-g111: 'per ch’io vo' is a fused expression (perchè / per ch’) meaning 'therefore' or 'so that' introducing the result/reason for the previous statement; 'vo' is the present of andare ('I go'), 'tra costor' = 'among these people' (tra = among), and 'con bassa fronte' is an idiom 'with lowered/downcast brow' (bassa modifies fronte).
segments.purgatorio-5-g112: E io a lui is a compact formula 'And I to him' used to introduce the speaker's question; word order puts the indirect object 'a lui' after 'io' and before the quoted question.
segments.purgatorio-5-g113: 'Qual forza o qual ventura' is a correlative pair 'what force or what fortune' as alternatives. 'ti travïò' is past-historic third-person singular with diaeresis marking the hiatus (travïò) meaning 'led/forced you away'; 'sì fuor di Campaldino' uses archaic fuor (fuori) 'out' + di + place (Campaldino) to indicate being driven away from that place.
segments.purgatorio-5-g114: This is a result/relative clause introduced by che: 'that your burial was never known.' 'non' negates the clause; 'si seppe' is an impersonal/passive construction (si + past of sapere) meaning 'was known'; 'mai' with the negative means 'never', and 'tua sepultura' = 'your burial'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g124: 'Quivi' is an adverb meaning 'there', placing the action. 'perdei' is the passato remoto (first-person singular) of 'perdere' — 'I lost' — the subject 'I' is carried by the verb and omitted. 'la vista e la parola' are two coordinated direct objects ('the sight and the speech'), each marked by the definite article.
segments.purgatorio-5-g125: 'nel' is the contraction of 'in + il' meaning 'in the' (here 'in the name of'). 'nome' is the noun 'name', 'di Maria' is the genitive 'of Mary'. 'fini’' (finì) is an archaic/poetic past tense form (finì) meaning 'ended' — here idiomatically 'I died' (the past action that concluded the narrator's life).
segments.purgatorio-5-g126: The conjunction 'e' links this clause to the previous. 'quivi' again is 'there' (adverb of place). 'caddi' is the passato remoto, first-person singular of 'cadere' — 'I fell' — a simple past narrative verb; the subject is again encoded in the verb.
segments.purgatorio-5-g127: 'e' continues the coordination. 'rimase' is the passato remoto / passato remoto-like third-person singular of 'rimanere' meaning 'remained' or 'was left'; here the verb precedes its subject for stylistic emphasis. 'la mia carne sola' is the subject noun phrase 'my flesh alone' (sola = 'alone/only'), meaning only his body remained.
segments.purgatorio-5-g128: 'Io' is the explicit subject 'I'. 'dirò' is the future tense, first-person singular of 'dire' — 'I will say' or 'I will tell'. 'vero' functions here as the object meaning 'the truth' (literally 'true' used substantively): 'I will tell the truth.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g129: 'e' links clauses. 'tu' is the second-person subject 'you'. ''l' ' is the clitic direct object 'it' (referring back to 'vero'). 'ridì' is an archaic spelling for the second-person singular present of 'ridere' — here 'you laugh' or 'you mock' — and 'tra 'vivi' ('among the living') is a locative phrase specifying where/among whom this mockery occurs.
segments.purgatorio-5-g130: 'l’angel' is 'the angel' (subject), 'di Dio' is a genitive 'of God' identifying which angel, 'mi' is the first-person object clitic 'me', and 'prese' is the passato remoto (third-person singular) of 'prendere' — 'took me' — narrating the action that occurred.
segments.purgatorio-5-g131: 'e' continues the narrative. 'quel d’inferno' literally 'that one of Hell' (i.e., the infernal spirit) is the subject of the next verb; 'gridava' is the imperfect tense (third-person singular of 'gridare') — 'he cried out' — which describes his ongoing cry at that moment.
segments.purgatorio-5-g132: This is a vocative/exclamation: 'O' (an interjection 'oh') calls attention; 'tu' addresses 'you'; 'del' = 'di + il' 'of the'; and 'ciel' (poetic form of 'cielo') means 'heaven' — 'O you of heaven' (an address to the angel).
segments.purgatorio-5-g133: 'perché' is the interrogative 'why' introducing the direct question; 'mi' is the clitic object 'me'; 'privi' is the second-person singular present (archaic form) of 'privare' — 'you deprive' — so the demon asks 'Why do you deprive me (of him)?'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g134: 'Tu' is the subject 'you'. 'te ne porti' uses the pronominal construction of 'portarsene' (here present indicative, second-person singular) meaning 'you carry away' or 'you take away' — 'te' is the reflexive/emphatic clitic and 'ne' is the particle related to 'of/from it/them'. 'di costui l’etterno' specifies what is taken: 'the eternal (thing) of this one' (i.e. his eternity). 'per una lagrimetta' 'by/for one little tear' — 'che 'l mi toglie' is a relative clause ('which takes him from me') where 'che' = 'which' and 'togli(e)' (third-person singular) 'takes away', with the clitics 'l'' (him/it) and 'mi' (me). Note the archaic spelling 'etterno' and the pronominal verbs.
segments.purgatorio-5-g135: 'ma' is the adversative 'but'. 'io farò' is first-person future of 'fare' — 'I will make/do'. 'de l’altro' ('de' = 'di') means 'concerning the other' or 'of the other'. 'altro altro' is a doubled adjective for emphasis — 'a different other' — and 'governo' is 'rule/governance' (here 'a different handling/administration' of the other soul). The line expresses the infernal speaker's threat to deal differently with another person.
segments.purgatorio-5-g136: A single period/full stop punctuation mark that ends the speech and the tercet; it has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-5-g39: This is a protasis introduced by Se ('if'). Per + infinitive (per veder) gives purpose/intent ('in order to see'). La sua ombra is the direct object of veder. Restaro is a 3rd-person plural past form (archaic Dantean spelling for 'restarono' / 'they stayed/ remained').
segments.purgatorio-5-g40: Com’ io avviso = 'as I think' (com’ = come/‘as’, io explicit subject). Avviso here means 'opinion' or 'I suppose' (Dante uses it to signal the speaker's conjecture). The compressed comma marks the comment inserted into the conditional.
segments.purgatorio-5-g41: Assai è lor risposto is an impersonal/passive-styled perfect: 'assai' = enough, è + risposto (past participle) means 'has been answered to them' or 'they have been answered sufficiently.' Lor is the dative 'to them.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g42: Fàccianli is an imperative/subjunctival third-person plural form (Dantean spelling of facciano) with the enclitic li 'to him' attached: 'let them do/pay him.' Onore is the noun object 'honor' (facciano onore a lui).
segments.purgatorio-5-g43: Ed = and; esser può is inverted order of modern può esser ('it may be'); lor = dative 'to them'; caro is a predicate adjective 'dear/pleasing.' So literally 'and it may be dear to them.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g44: Vapori accesi = 'kindled vapors' (past participle used adjectivally). Non vid' io = negative inversion with the clitic omission (vid' = vidi), 'I did not see'; sì tosto = 'so soon'; di prima notte = 'of the first/early night'; mai fender sereno = 'ever split the clear sky' (mai with non yields 'never'). Fender is an infinitive 'to split/cleave' and sereno is 'clear sky/serenity.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g45: Né = 'nor' (continues the negative thought started by non ... mai).
segments.purgatorio-5-g46: Sol calando = sol (short for solo = merely/just) + gerund calando 'descending/setting' (of clouds or the sun), i.e. 'even when merely setting.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g47: Nuvole d’agosto = 'clouds of August' (d' = di). The phrase names a typical summer cloud-form.
segments.purgatorio-5-g48: Che introduces a relative/result clause: 'that those did not return up in less (i.e. without being diminished)'. Color is an archaic plural demonstrative 'those' referring to the vapors/clouds; tornasser is an older subjunctival/imperfect form (tornassero/tornarono) meaning 'returned' or 'came back'; suso = 'up'; in meno = 'with less/ diminished.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g49: E = and, linking the previous meteorological image to the following action.
segments.purgatorio-5-g50: Giunti là is a past participle phrase 'having arrived there' (giunti = reached, là = there), elliptical subject 'they' understood from the preceding clouds/vapors.
segments.purgatorio-5-g51: Con li altri a noi dier volta = 'with the others they turned toward us.' Con li altri = 'with the others'; a noi = dative 'to us'; dier volta is an archaic past-tense idiom (dier ≈ diedero/they gave, volta = 'turn') meaning 'they turned.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g52: Come schiera che scorre sanza freno is a simile: come = 'like/as'; schiera = 'host/troop'; che scorre = 'that flows/passes by'; sanza (archaic) = 'without'; freno = 'rein/restraint' — 'like a troop streaming on without restraint.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g53: Questa gente = 'this people' (gente is a singular, collective noun); che preme a noi = relative clause 'that presses upon/against us' (preme takes a dative of person here: a noi); è molta = 'is many/large' (molta agrees with gente — feminine singular meaning 'numerous').
segments.purgatorio-5-g54: E vegnonti a pregar = 'and they come to entreat/pray.' Vegnonti is an archaic present/plural form (modern vengono/vegnono) meaning 'they come/are coming'; a + infinitive marks purpose ('to entreat').
segments.purgatorio-5-g55: Disse ’l poeta = 'said the poet.' Disse is the past (remote) 'he said'; ’l is the contracted article the poeta follows ('the poet').
segments.purgatorio-5-g56: Però pur va = 'therefore/still go' (però = therefore/yet; pur = still/even so); va here is an imperative or exhortation 'go on' addressed to the pilgrim — short, colloquial instruction.
segments.purgatorio-5-g57: E in andando ascolta = 'and while going, listen.' In andando = gerundial phrase 'in going/while you go'; ascolta is an imperative 'listen' (address to the same person told to go).
segments.purgatorio-5-g58: Vocative O anima = 'O soul' addressing the soul directly. Che vai = relative/verb 'who go' (2nd-person present, since the soul is being spoken to). Per esser lieta = purpose 'to be happy'; con quelle membra con le quai nascesti = 'with those limbs with which you were born' — quai is an archaic form of quali ('which'), and nascesti is 2nd-person singular past ('you were born'), appropriate because the soul is spoken to directly.
segments.purgatorio-5-g59: Venian gridando = imperfect 'they were coming' (venian = venivano) + gerund gridando 'shouting/crying out'; it describes the crowd's action as they call out the vocative line.
segments.purgatorio-5-g60: Un poco il passo queta is an exhortation: 'a little, quiet the pace' (un poco = 'a little'; il passo = 'the step/pace'; queta is imperative/verb form meaning 'calm/slow' — here addressing the addressee to slow their pace). The verb form queta is archaic/poetic for 'quiet'/'still'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g83: Short introductory clause: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. 'uno' is an indefinite pronoun meaning 'someone' or 'one' (here 'one' as an unspecified person). 'incominciò' is the 3rd-person singular passato remoto of incominciare, 'he/she/it began', used to report a speech act.
segments.purgatorio-5-g84: 'Ciascun' is an indefinite pronoun meaning 'each' or 'everyone' and works as the subject. 'si' is the reflexive/impersonal clitic used here with 'fida' to mean 'trusts' or 'places trust' (impersonal/reciprocal sense). 'fida' is present indicative (3rd sg) of fidare, 'trusts' or 'relies on'. 'del' is the contraction di+il = 'of the'. 'beneficio tuo' = 'your favor/kindness' (beneficio = favor/grace here). 'sanza' is the older form of 'senza' meaning 'without'. 'giurarlo' is the infinitive 'to swear it' (giurare + accusative clitic -lo). The clause is an independent assertion introducing the content of the speech.
segments.purgatorio-5-g85: 'pur che' is a conditional concessive phrase meaning roughly 'provided that' or 'so long as'. '’l' is the article 'il' before the noun 'voler' (voler = 'will/desire'). 'nonpossa' is the fused negative + present subjunctive of potere (non possa = 'cannot' in a subordinate proviso clause). The double negative 'non ... non' frames 'ricida', an archaic/adjectival/verb form whose exact meaning is unclear here; the clause therefore places a condition on the preceding statement (trust is permitted provided that ...).'
segments.purgatorio-5-g86: 'Ond’' is the contracted form of 'ond' or 'onde' meaning 'therefore' or 'from that', used to link this speaker's turn to what came before. 'io' is the subject 'I'. This is a left dislocation placing the pronoun before the relative clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-5-g87: Relative clause introduced by 'che' ('who/that') modifying 'io'. 'solo' = 'alone'; 'innanzi a li altri' = 'before the others' (innanzi + preposition a + plural article 'li' + 'altri'). 'parlo' should be read as 'parlo' (1st sg present of parlare, 'I speak'), though the lemma in the list is odd; here it means 'I speak' and completes the relative clause 'I, who alone speak before the others'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g88: 'Ti' is the unstressed object pronoun 'to you'. 'priego' is 1st-person present of 'priegare' (to beg/entreat) and here means 'I beg you' or 'I ask you' — a polite request to the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-5-g89: Conditional temporal clause: 'se mai' = 'if ever'. 'vedi' is 2nd-person present of 'vedere' ('you see'). 'quel paese' = 'that district/country'. The relative 'che siede' ('which sits' or 'which lies') uses 'siede' (present of sedere) in the sense 'is located'. 'tra Romagna e quel di Carlo' = 'between Romagna and that of Charles' (quel di = 'the territory/land of').
segments.purgatorio-5-g90: Content clause with 'che' = 'that': 'that you be' — 'tu' (you) + 'mi' (to me) + 'sie' (old subjunctive form of 'sia', from essere) gives 'that you be'. 'di tuoi prieghi cortese in Fano' = 'courteous/kind toward me by your entreaties/prayers in Fano' (di + tuoi prieghi = 'with your entreaties/prayers'). Note: 'sie' is an archaic subjunctive form used here to express a request or wish.
segments.purgatorio-5-g91: 'sì che' introduces a purposive/result clause 'so that'. 'ben per me s’adori' = 'I may be well honored/regarded on account of me' — 's’adori' is an impersonal/passive subjunctive (si + adori) meaning 'may be honored'. 'pur ch’ i’ possa purgar le gravi offese' = 'provided that I may be able to purge/cleanse the serious offenses' — 'possa' is present subjunctive of potere expressing possibility/condition, and 'purgar' is the infinitive 'to purge'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g92: 'Quindi' = 'then/from there/so' linking to the previous action. 'fu’' (fui) is passato remoto of essere 'was' or 'I went (from there)' in narrative; followed by the subject pronoun 'io' for emphasis. This is a concise narrative clause marking the speaker's movement away.
segments.purgatorio-5-g93: Contrastive clause introduced by 'ma' = 'but'. 'li profondi fóri' = 'the deep holes/pits' (the provided lemmas are a bit inconsistent; here they function as plural nouns). 'ond’ uscì 'l sangue' = 'from which the blood came out' ('ond'' = 'whence'). 'in sul quale io sedea' = 'onto the surface/ground on which I was sitting' (sedea = imperfect of sedere, 'I was seated').
segments.purgatorio-5-g94: 'fatti mi fuoro' is a terse medieval/periphrastic passive expression: 'were made for me' / 'were made to me' — 'fatti' (past participle 'made'), 'mi' indirect object 'for me', 'fuoro' (passive auxiliary-like, variant of furono) yields 'they were made'. 'in grembo a li Antenori' = 'in the lap/bosom of the Antenors' (Antenori is a proper name/group). The phrase locates who the makers/hosts were.
segments.purgatorio-5-g95: 'là dov’ io più sicuro esser credea' = 'there where I believed myself more secure'. 'là' = 'there'; 'dov’' is contracted 'dove' = 'where'; 'io' subject; 'più sicuro' = 'more safe/secure'; 'esser credea' = imperfect 'crede(a)' with infinitive 'esser' (archaic word order: 'I believed myself to be more secure').
segments.purgatorio-5-g96: 'quel da Esti' = 'that one/from Este' (a man of Este). 'il fé far' = 'had it made' — 'fé' = past 'fece' (he made/caused), followed by the infinitive 'far' (to make/do) used to indicate causation ('had ... made'). The sequence attributes agency to a man of Este.
segments.purgatorio-5-g97: 'che' introduces a relative clause describing that man: 'm’avea in ira' = 'he had me in anger' (i.e., he bore anger against me). 'assai più là che dritto non volea' = 'much more than was right' — 'assai' 'very/much', 'più là' 'far beyond', 'che dritto non volea' literally 'than right he did not wish' meaning 'more than was just/proper'. Verb 'avea' is imperfect of avere conveying ongoing past attitude.
segments.purgatorio-5-g98: 'Ma' = 'but' introducing a counterfactual conditional. 's’io fosse fuggito' is the protasis of a past counterfactual: 'if I had fled' — 'fosse' is the imperfect subjunctive of essere used with the past participle 'fuggito' (classical-style pluperfect subjunctive in a conditional). 'inver’ la Mira' = 'toward/in the direction of the Mira' (inver = 'toward/against' in older usage; Mira is a place-name).
segments.purgatorio-5-g99: 'quando' = 'when'. 'fu’ sovragiunto' = 'I/he was overtaken' — 'fu' (passato remoto of essere) + 'sovragiunto' (past participle of sovragiungere) yields 'was overtaken/came upon'. 'ad Orïaco' = 'by Orïaco' (Orïaco is a person/place that overran the speaker). This clause gives the time/circumstance of the hypothetical flight.
segments.purgatorio-5-g100: 'ancor sarei' = 'I would still be' (conditional present: sarei = conditional of essere). 'di là' = 'from there' / 'over there'. 'dove si spira' = 'where one breathes' or 'where people live/where the air is breathed' — 'si' is the impersonal/reflexive pronoun and 'spira' is present indicative of spirare, 'breathe'/'blow'. The whole is the apodosis of the conditional expressing the contrary-to-fact result.
segments.purgatorio-5-g137: Ben sai = 'you know well': ben is an adverb placed before the verb sai (2nd person singular present of sapere). come introduces the subordinate clause 'how / in what way'. ne l’aere is an older word order for nel/aere ('in the air'), with the pronoun ne + the article l' before aere. si raccoglie is a present, impersonal/reflexive construction meaning 'gathers' or 'is gathered' (si marks the passive/impersonal). quell’ umido vapor is the subject of that subordinate clause and che in acqua riede is a relative clause: riede (from riedere) means 'returns' or 'reverts' to water.
segments.purgatorio-5-g138: tosto che = 'as soon as' (tosto + conjunction che). sale is 3rd person singular present of salire ('it/it rises'); dove introduces the locative relative 'where'. 'l freddo is the subject 'the cold', and il before coglie is the clitic object pronoun 'it' referring back to the vapor; coglie (3rd person present of cogliere) means 'seizes' or 'takes hold of'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g139: Giunse is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of giungere, 'arrived' or 'came'. quel mal voler is a nominal phrase: 'that bad will/wish' (voler as noun = will/desire). che pur mal chiede is a relative clause: che = 'which', pur = 'still' (or 'even'), mal chiede = 'asks/demands evil' (chiede in present tense). con lo ’ntelletto marks the manner or instrument 'with the intellect' (lo 'ntelletto is the elided / poetic form of intelletto).
segments.purgatorio-5-g140: e mosse = 'and (it) moved / set in motion': mosse is passato remoto of muovere. il fummo e ’l vento = 'the smoke and the wind' (fummo is an archaic/spelling variant of fumo). per la virtù = 'by/through the power/force'; che sua natura diede is a relative clause 'which its nature gave' (diede = passato remoto of dare).
segments.purgatorio-5-g141: Indi = 'then' or 'thence'; la valle = 'the valley' (subject or topic). The phrase simply sets the temporal/location scene: 'Then the valley...'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g142: come ’l dì fu spento = 'as/when the day was spent': come used temporally 'when/as'; ’l dì = 'the day'; fu spento is passato remoto (essere + participle spento) = 'was extinguished' i.e. night fell.
segments.purgatorio-5-g143: da Pratomagno al gran giogo coperse = 'it covered (them) from Pratomagno to the great ridge': da ... al indicates the span; coperse is passato remoto of coprire ('covered'), and di nebbia ('with fog') expresses what covered it.
segments.purgatorio-5-g144: e ’l ciel di sopra fece intento = 'and the sky above became intent/attentive': fece (passato remoto of fare) + predicate intento (attentive/intent) describes a change of state in the sky 'above'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g145: sì che ’l pregno aere in acqua si converse = 'so that the moisture-laden air was turned into water': sì che introduces a result clause; 'pregno aere' = 'air full of moisture'; in acqua si converse uses si + verb to indicate a change/state (here 'was converted into water').
segments.purgatorio-5-g146: la pioggia cadde = simple past statement 'the rain fell': cadde is passato remoto of cadere.
segments.purgatorio-5-g147: e a’ fossati venne di lei ciò che la terra non sofferse = 'and into the ditches came from it that which the earth could not endure': a' fossati = to the ditches, venne = 'came', di lei refers back to the rain, e ciò che = 'that which', la terra non sofferse = 'the earth did not bear/endure' (so the water ran off into ditches).
segments.purgatorio-5-g148: e come ai rivi grandi si convenne = 'and as was fitting for the great streams': come = 'as', ai rivi grandi = 'to/for the large streams', si convenne is impersonal/passive sense 'it was fitting / it had to happen'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g149: ver’ lo fiume real tanto veloce si ruinò = 'toward the royal river it rushed so swiftly': ver' is an archaic contraction of verso/'toward' (here 'toward'), lo fiume real = 'the royal river', tanto veloce modifies the verb, and si ruinò (passato remoto of ruinare) describes a violent rushing or falling of water toward the river.
segments.purgatorio-5-g150: che nulla la ritenne = 'so that nothing held it back': che introduces the consequence/result, nulla = 'nothing', la = clitic object pronoun 'it' (the flood), and ritenne is passato remoto of ritenere 'held back / retained'.
segments.purgatorio-5-g151: Lo corpo mio gelato in su la foce places the patient phrase (my body, frozen) before the verb for poetic emphasis. Trovò is passato remoto (third person singular) meaning “found,” and the true subject (l’Archian rubesto) follows the verb — a common inversion in Italian poetry. Gelato is a past participle used adjectivally; l’ is the elided article before a vowel.
segments.purgatorio-5-g152: e quel sospinse ne l’Arno is a short clause where quel (that one) resumes the previously named agent; sospinse is passato remoto (3rd sg) 'pushed/thrust'. The particle ne here means 'into' (ne + l' = nella/nel in older/poetic usage), so sospinse ne l’Arno = 'pushed it into the Arno.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g153: e sciolse al mio petto la croce places the verb sciolse (passato remoto of sciogliere, 'loosened/untied') before its object; al mio petto indicates the locus ('at/on my breast') where the cross was. The relative clause ch’i’ fe’ di me uses archaic contractions: ch’ = che ('that/which'), i’ = io ('I') and fe’ is an archaic contraction of feci ('I made'), so 'the cross which I made of myself.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g154: voltòmmi per le ripe e per lo fondo contains the past-tense verb voltòmmi, which is voltò + the enclitic pronoun mi ('it/ he turned me'). The verbo in passato remoto (third person) plus enclitic indicates the river as agent turning the speaker. Per le ripe e per lo fondo is a series of prepositional phrases meaning 'along the banks and along the bed/bottom.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g155: poi di sua preda mi coperse e cinse combines two passato remoto verbs (coperse from coprire, 'covered'; cinse from cingere, 'encircled/closed around') with mi as the direct object enclitic ('me'). Di sua preda indicates instrument or cause ('with his prey' / 'by means of his spoil'), and poi simply means 'then.'
segments.purgatorio-5-g156: Deh is an archaic interjection used to introduce a plea or entreaty (like "ah" or "oh, please"). It stands alone before the temporal clause that follows and signals the speaker's appeal.
segments.purgatorio-5-g157: This is a temporal clause introduced by quando ("when"). Sarai tornato is the future perfect (future of essere + past participle), literally "you will have returned," and tornato agrees as a past participle; riposato repeats the same perfect idea by ellipsis (the auxiliary sarai is understood to govern both tornato and riposato). De is an archaic form of di ("from/of"), and la lunga via = "the long road/journey."
segments.purgatorio-5-g158: Seguitò is passato remoto (simple past) of seguitare and here means "continued" or "followed (in speaking)"; ’l is the contracted article il ("the"). The phrase literally reads "the third spirit continued to the second," i.e., the third spirit continued speaking directed at the second spirit.
segments.purgatorio-5-g159: Ricorditi is the second-person singular imperative with the clitic -ti attached (modern ricordati), meaning "remember"; the preposition di introduces the person to be remembered (di me = "me"). The imperative form is direct address to the listener.
segments.purgatorio-5-g160: Che here is a relative pronoun meaning "who"; son is the contracted poetic form of sono (first-person singular present "I am"). La Pia is an epithet/name (literally "the Pia"), so the clause reads "who am Pia."
segments.purgatorio-5-g161: This is a short clause with subject first: Siena (the city) as subject, fé is the passato remoto of fare (made, caused), and mi is the direct-object clitic "me." The word order is typical of poetry, placing the subject up front for emphasis: "Siena made me."
segments.purgatorio-5-g162: Disfecemi is a fused past-tense verb + clitic (disfece + mi) meaning "undid/destroyed me" (passato remoto), with the subject Maremma following the verb — an inversion used for contrast with the previous line: "Maremma undid me."
segments.purgatorio-5-g163: This clause centers on a relative clause introduced by colui che ("the one who"). ’Nnanellata is a past participle (poetic elision of annellata, "ringed/encircled with a ring") and pria means "before/previously." Disposando is a present participle/gerund-like form here meaning "having arranged/disposing," and m’avea is the auxiliary avea ("had") with the clitic m' (mi) contracted (mi aveva = "had me"); con la sua gemma is an instrument/means phrase "with his jewel." Note: some individual lexical forms in this clause are archaic or elided poetic forms and the participial constructions stack to describe something the man had done earlier.

sentences.purgatorio-5-s17: Look whether you ever saw any of us, so that you might bring news of him from there; ah, why are you going?
sentences.purgatorio-5-s19: We had all already been killed by force, and were sinners up to the very last hour; there the light of heaven made us aware of it, so that, repenting and being forgiven, we went forth from life reconciled to God, who is grieved by the desire to behold himself.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s20: And I: "Because in the looks on your faces I recognize no one; but if it pleases you, well-born spirits, that there be anything I can (do), you say it, and I will do it for that peace which, following at the feet of such a guide, makes me seek from world to world."
sentences.purgatorio-5-s1: I had already left those shades and was following the traces of my guide, when from behind me, pointing his finger, someone cried: "Look — does it not seem that the ray shines from the left toward that lower place, and how lively it seems to be guided!"
sentences.purgatorio-5-s3: I turned my eyes at the sound of that utterance, and I saw them look, through wonder, even at me — even at me — and at the light that had been broken.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s4: "Why does your spirit get so entangled," said the master, "that it slackens your walking?"
sentences.purgatorio-5-s31: “Oh!” he answered, “at the foot of the Casentino there runs a stream that is called the Archiano.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s32: There, where its name becomes meaningless, I came, pierced, into the throat, fleeing on foot and bleeding across the plain.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s5: What makes you do what is whispered here?
sentences.purgatorio-5-s6: Come after me, and let the people speak: stand like a steady tower, whose summit never falls because of the blowing of winds; for whenever a man's thought sprouts thought upon thought, he draws the mark away from himself, because ardour suns one upon another.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s7: What could I reply, except 'I come'?
sentences.purgatorio-5-s8: He said it, somewhat of the color sprinkled that sometimes makes a man deserving of pardon.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s9: And meanwhile, people were coming across the slope a little ahead of us, singing 'Miserere' verse by verse.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s10: When they perceived that I did not make room with my body for the passing of the rays, they changed their song into an 'oh!'
sentences.purgatorio-5-s11: Long and hoarse; and two of them, in the form of messengers, ran toward us and asked about it: 'Make them wise of your condition.'
sentences.purgatorio-5-s12: And my master: 'You can go and tell those who sent you that this man's body is true flesh.'
sentences.purgatorio-5-s25: I ran to the marsh, and the reeds and the hound entangled me so that I fell; and there I saw my veins become a pool on the ground.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s26: Then another said: 'Ah, if that desire which draws you to the high mountain may be fulfilled, with good compassion help mine!'
sentences.purgatorio-5-s27: I was of Montefeltro, I am Bonconte; Giovanna or others care nothing for me; so I go among these people with downcast brow.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s28: And I to him: 'What force or what fortune drove you so far out of Campaldino, that your burial was never known?'
sentences.purgatorio-5-s33: There I lost my sight and my speech; in the name of Mary I died, and there I fell, and only my flesh remained.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s34: I will tell the truth, and you will mock it among the living: the angel of God took me, and that one from Hell cried, "O you of heaven, why do you deprive me?"
sentences.purgatorio-5-s35: You carry off from this one his eternity by a single little tear that takes him from me; but I will make a different rule concerning the other!
sentences.purgatorio-5-s13: If they stayed behind to see his shadow, as I suppose, they have already been answered enough: let them pay him honor, and it may be pleasing to them.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s14: I had never seen vapors so kindled so soon split the clear sky of the early night, nor (when they were merely sinking) clouds of August that did not afterwards come back up diminished; and, having reached there, they turned toward us with the others, like a host that runs on without a rein.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s15: “This people that presses against us is many, and they come to entreat,” said the poet: “so go on, and as you go, listen.”
sentences.purgatorio-5-s16: “O soul who go to be happy with those limbs with which you were born,” they cried out, “a little — quiet your pace.”
sentences.purgatorio-5-s21: And one began: "Everyone trusts in your favor without swearing it, provided that the will cannot fall back."
sentences.purgatorio-5-s22: So I, who alone speak before the others, beg you — if you ever see that district which lies between Romagna and that of Charles — that you be courteous to me with your entreaties in Fano, so that I may be well regarded for my sake, provided that I may be able to purge the grave offenses.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s23: Then I went from there; but the deep pits from which the blood issued onto the ground where I sat were made for me in the lap of the Antenors, there where I believed myself more secure: a man from Este had it made, who bore me anger far beyond what was right.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s24: But if I had fled toward the Mira when I was overtaken by Orïaco, I would still be over there where one breathes.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s37: You know well how, in the air, that damp vapor which returns to water gathers, as soon as it rises to where the cold seizes it.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s38: That evil will came, which still asks for evil with the intellect, and set the smoke and the wind in motion by the power that its nature gave.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s39: Then the valley, when the day was spent, was covered with mist from Pratomagno to the great ridge; and the sky above became intent, so that the moisture-laden air was turned into water; the rain fell, and into the ditches came from it what the earth could not endure; and, as was fitting for the large streams, toward the royal river it rushed so swiftly that nothing held it back.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s40: The mighty Arno found my frozen body at the river-mouth; he thrust it into the Arno and loosened from my breast the cross that I had made of myself when pain overcame me; he rolled me along the banks and along the bed, then covered me with his prey and clasped me.
sentences.purgatorio-5-s41: "Ah, when you shall have returned to the world and rested from the long journey," the third spirit continued to the second, "remember me, I am Pia; Siena made me, Maremma undid me: he it was who, having first slipped a ring on (me), had disposed of me with his jewel."
